title: Origami lotus flower
subtitle: Folding a sixteen-petal lotus from two square sheets nested together
excerpt: The origami lotus is made from two square sheets folded identically and nested together with the petal layers offset. Each sheet produces eight petal points that are peeled outward in sequence to open the flower. The model uses only valley folds and is suitable for 15 cm kami.
sourceNotes: The origami lotus is a widely taught traditional model of indeterminate origin, consistent with the Japanese modular origami tradition. The two-unit nesting method described here is the standard home-practice version documented across origami manuals and craft guides from the late 20th century onward.

glossaryTerms.definition: The most basic origami fold: the paper is folded toward you, creating a crease that forms a V-shape (a valley) when viewed from the side. The fold line goes into the paper away from the viewer. Shown in diagrams with a dashed line.

In the lotus model, the action of gently peeling the corner points of the folded base outward and downward to form petals. This is done after all folding is complete, working from the innermost layer outward. The points curl back as the petals open.

content.text: and one peel-open action at the end. Two identical folded squares are nested to produce sixteen petal points total. Use two colours or two shades for the most visually effective result: the inner layer shows at the centre, the outer layer forms the outer petals.

items.name: Mini bone folder (origami) (optional, for sharp creases)

content.text: Use two 15 cm squares: one for the outer base and one for the inner. Thinner paper gives finer petals; heavier paper makes a sturdier model that holds its shape better on display.
Folding each square (repeat for both sheets)

Place the sheet colour-side down. Fold the bottom edge to the top edge (valley fold). Crease and unfold. Fold the left edge to the right edge. Crease and unfold. You now have a cross-crease grid: two folds dividing the sheet into four equal squares.
Fold all four corners of the sheet in to meet the centre point. Crease each fold flat. The result is a smaller square with all four corners meeting at the centre, colour-side now showing.
Fold all four corners in to the centre point again. The sheet is now one quarter of its original size. Crease all folds sharply.
Turn the model over (white or plain side up). Fold all four corners in to the centre point on this face. Crease.
Keep the model in this position. Fold each of the four corners in to the centre once more. This is the final fold on this face. Crease all four firmly.

Place the two finished folded squares back-to-back with the plain side of one facing the plain side of the other. Rotate one 45 degrees so the corners of the two squares alternate around the circumference. Hold together firmly at the centre.
Turn the combined model colour-side up. Working from the outermost layer, gently pull each of the eight corner points outward and back using the
action: curl the tip downward and away from the centre to form a petal. Work through all eight outer points.
Work inward to the second layer: pull each of the eight inner points outward and back to form the smaller inner petals. Adjust all sixteen petals until the flower is symmetrical.
